title: Spaghetti al tonno
subtitle: Tinned tuna, capers, and tomato over spaghetti
excerpt: A pantry pasta that takes fifteen minutes from opening tins to sitting down. Good tinned tuna in olive oil, capers, and a short cook with cherry tomatoes — no cream, no fuss. The kind of thing a Roman household makes on a weeknight without thinking.
sourceNotes: Pasta al tonno appears across southern Italian and Sicilian home cooking, documented in regional variations by Pellegrino Artusi, La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain). The combination of tinned tuna, capers, and tomato is particularly common in Naples and along the Tyrrhenian coast, where tinned fish has been a kitchen staple since the early twentieth century.

content.text: The quality of the tuna matters here more than in almost any other pantry recipe. Tinned tuna packed in olive oil has a texture and flavour that tuna packed in brine or sunflower oil cannot match. The oil drains into the sauce and carries the fish flavour through every strand. Buy the best you can manage.

There are two versions: rosso (with tomato) and bianco (without). This is the rosso, built on cherry tomatoes and a little passata. The bianco uses just garlic, olive oil, parsley, and perhaps a few capers — equally good, three minutes faster.

items.prepNote: in olive oil, drained (two 140 g tins)

content.text: Bring a large pan of well-salted water to a boil. Add the {{pasta-dried}} of spaghetti and cook until 2 minutes short of the packet's al dente time. Scoop out 150 ml of pasta water before draining.

While the pasta cooks, warm the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and fry for 1 minute until pale gold. Add the chilli flakes if using. Add the {{cherry-tomatoes}} of cherry tomatoes and the {{capers}} of capers. Cook for 5 minutes until the tomatoes collapse and release their juice. Break in the {{tuna-tinned}} of tuna in large flakes and stir once — you want chunks, not mush.

Add the drained pasta to the frying pan with a splash of pasta water. Toss over medium heat for 1 to 2 minutes until the sauce coats the pasta. Add the parsley, check seasoning, and divide between warm bowls.

items.symptom: Tuna is dry and flaky rather than moist
items.cause: Overworked in the pan, or the tin was packed in brine rather than oil
items.fix: Break the tuna in at the last moment and stir only once; oil-packed tuna retains moisture far better

items.cause: Capers not rinsed, or tomatoes were underripe and acidic
items.fix: Rinse capers thoroughly; add a pinch of sugar to balance very acidic tomatoes

items.cause: Not enough pasta water, or pasta sat too long after draining
items.fix: Keep the pasta water close and add it in 2-tablespoon increments while tossing

items.cause: Capers added but not enough, or the pasta water was under-salted
items.fix: Taste before serving; the pasta water should taste recognisably salty before the pasta goes in

content.text: Pasta al tonno is a weeknight staple across Italy's coastline, from Naples to Palermo to the Ligurian ports. It became widespread in the mid-twentieth century as tinned tuna moved from luxury to household staple. The version without tomato is older; the version with tomato arrived as passata canning industrialised. Both remain in circulation, and families argue about which is correct with the same conviction they apply to carbonara and cream.
